unit.name: Encoder pretraining and BERT
unit.description: Covers architecture-specific pretraining for encoders, explains masked language modeling, and introduces BERT’s training recipe and its impact on NLP.
unit.summary: The lecture next organizes pretraining by architecture—encoders, encoder-decoders, and decoders—and begins with encoders, which have bidirectional context and therefore cannot use ordinary left-to-right language modeling without trivially peeking at the answer [ts=2155s] [ts=2241s]. The workaround is masked language modeling: corrupt the input by blanking selected tokens, run the encoder over the full masked sequence, and compute loss only on the masked positions so the model learns to reconstruct missing words from both left and right context [ts=2269s] [ts=2327s]. This leads into BERT, introduced as the model that popularized masked language modeling and released pretrained transformer weights that became broadly useful for representation learning [ts=2448s] [ts=2470s]. BERT masks 15% of subword tokens, but mixes masking, random replacement, and unchanged tokens so the network learns to build contextual representations even when no explicit mask appears at test time [ts=2483s] [ts=2571s]. The segment also explains the next-sentence prediction task with segment embeddings, notes that later work found it unnecessary, and highlights how BERT-style pretraining outperformed many bespoke task-specific architectures on standard NLP benchmarks [ts=2608s] [ts=3005s].

key_points.text: Encoders cannot use plain next-word language modeling for pretraining because bidirectional attention leaks the target token.
Masked language modeling trains an encoder to reconstruct masked tokens from both left and right context.
BERT masks 15% of subword tokens and uses a mixture of true masks, random replacements, and unchanged tokens.
Next-sentence prediction asks whether segment B is the true continuation of segment A.
Longer contexts help contextual interpretation, which is one reason later work found next-sentence prediction unnecessary.
BERT-style pretraining largely displaced hand-crafted task-specific architectures across benchmark tasks.
